med_suggestions.dosing: 100 mg by mouth twice daily for 5 days (minimum; extend to 7 days if slow improvement)
med_suggestions.sig: 100 mg by mouth twice daily for 5 days (minimum; extend to 7 days if slow improvement)
med_suggestions.exclusions: Pregnancy/possible pregnancy; severe liver disease; allergy/intolerance

med_suggestions.dosing: 500 mg by mouth on day 1, then 250 mg by mouth daily on days 2–5
med_suggestions.sig: 500 mg by mouth on day 1, then 250 mg by mouth daily on days 2–5
med_suggestions.exclusions: QT prolongation history or on QT-prolonging meds; significant liver disease; allergy/intolerance; use only if locally appropriate

med_suggestions.dosing: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO twice daily for 5–7 days PLUS azithromycin 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg daily days 2–5
med_suggestions.sig: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO twice daily for 5–7 days PLUS azithromycin 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg daily days 2–5
med_suggestions.exclusions: Severe beta-lactam anaphylaxis; QT risk (for azithro); renal dose adjustment may be needed in CKD

note.soap:
S:
Patient is a 45-year-old adult who reports concern for pneumonia. There is no breathing trouble or chest pain. The patient is not considered high risk and has no medication allergies. The patient has a medication exclusion of QT prolongation.

O:
No physical exam or vital signs available due to phone-only history.

A:
 - Working diagnosis (most likely): Possible pneumonia
 - Differential:
  1. Viral respiratory infection
  2. Atypical pneumonia
  3. Bronchitis
  4. Other lower respiratory tract infection
 - Red flags considered: No breathing difficulty, no chest pain, no high-risk features reported.

P:
 - Disposition: Patient advised to seek in-person evaluation if symptoms worsen or new symptoms develop.
 - Plan: Given absence of breathing difficulty and chest pain, and no high-risk features, initial conservative management is reasonable. If clinical suspicion for bacterial pneumonia increases or patient develops worsening symptoms, further evaluation and treatment may be needed.
  - Primary medication option (based on the patient's responses): Doxycycline — 100 mg by mouth twice daily for 5 days (minimum; extend to 7 days if slow improvement)
   Medication protocol (from data/protocols/meds):
   - Medication recommendations (Tier 1):
   - Reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated.
   - Amoxicillin-clavulanate + Azithromycin — Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO twice daily for 5–7 days PLUS azithromycin 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg daily days 2–5
     - Exclusions: Severe beta-lactam anaphylaxis; QT risk (for azithro); renal dose adjustment may be needed in CKD
   - Doxycycline — 100 mg by mouth twice daily for 5 days (minimum; extend to 7 days if slow improvement)
     - Exclusions: Pregnancy/possible pregnancy; severe liver disease; allergy/intolerance
   - Azithromycin — 500 mg by mouth on day 1, then 250 mg by mouth daily on days 2–5
     - Exclusions: QT prolongation history or on QT-prolonging meds; significant liver disease; allergy/intolerance; use only if locally appropriate
   - Supportive care:
   - Hydration and rest.
   - Acetaminophen for fever/pain if safe for the patient.
   - Avoid NSAIDs if kidney disease, ulcer/GI bleed history, or blood thinners.
   - Follow-up and safety:
   - Follow-up: Recheck within 24–48 hours if started antibiotics; sooner if worsening.
   - Expected course: Some improvement by 48–72 hours if bacterial and treated; cough can last longer.
   - Go to urgent care/ED now if: Trouble breathing, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, fainting, or rapidly worsening symptoms.
   - In-person evaluation needed if: No improvement by 72 hours, persistent high fever, inability to keep fluids down, or concern for dehydration.
 - Safety net: Instruct patient to seek urgent care or emergency services if they develop breathing difficulty, chest pain, confusion, or worsening symptoms. Follow-up if symptoms persist beyond several days or worsen.
patient_instructions: Acetaminophen for fever/pain if safe for the patient.
Avoid NSAIDs if kidney disease, ulcer/GI bleed history, or blood thinners.
Follow-up: Recheck within 24–48 hours if started antibiotics; sooner if worsening.
Expected course: Some improvement by 48–72 hours if bacterial and treated; cough can last longer.
Go to urgent care/ED now if: Trouble breathing, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, fainting, or rapidly worsening symptoms.
In-person evaluation needed if: No improvement by 72 hours, persistent high fever, inability to keep fluids down, or concern for dehydration.
